title: MIT Center for Transportation & Logistics — Sustainable Trucking
full_brief:
# What MIT CTL's Greenest Path Research Means for Sustainable Trucking Operations

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises the findings of MIT Center for Transportation & Logistics (CTL) research on fuel-efficient routing for heavy trucks, anchored in the working paper "Load Asymptotics and Dynamic Speed Optimization for the Greenest Path Problem: A Comprehensive Analysis" (Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, arXiv:2306.01687, v2 revised 30 May 2025). Two corroborating sources were reachable and used: the MIT Sustainable Transportation landing page and the arXiv preprint record. Two corroborating sources were unreachable at generation time (MIT CTL publication page returned 404; University of Luxembourg City Logistics Freight Lab returned 403) and are listed under New Sources Identified as retrieval leads only — no claims are drawn from them. The Supply Chain Management Review article (Caplice & Velázquez-Martínez, 2025) and the MIT Climate Portal explainer are listed as identified leads; their content was not available in the source blocks provided and is therefore carried only as labeled inference where relevant.

Scope note: This is a pre-publication preprint revised through May 2025, not yet a formally peer-reviewed journal article in final form. The Related DOI field on arXiv points to a Springer OR journal record (doi.org/10.1007/s00291-024-00793-9), which may indicate a published version exists; that URL was not in the source blocks and its status is a research gap. The brief covers: (1) what the research found, (2) operational relevance for workspaces operating road freight, (3) what it changes for routing strategy and emissions claims, (4) client-conversation guidance, and (5) what it does not resolve.

*Severity: MONITORING — the research programme is active, v2 was revised in May 2025, and downstream regulatory use of terrain-aware emissions modelling is a developing area. Re-check window: Q4 2025 for journal publication confirmation and any regulatory citation of CMEM-variant methodologies.*

---

## 1. What the Research Found

**The headline finding.** FACT: "the greenest path quickly diverges from the shortest path and attains the asymptotic greenest path even for moderate payloads." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

In plain terms: for loaded trucks operating on roads with meaningful elevation change, the shortest route and the lowest-CO2 route are different routes — and the divergence sets in well before a truck reaches full load. A freight forwarder or road operator routing on shortest-distance or fastest-time criteria is not routing for minimum fuel consumption when terrain is non-flat.

**The model.** FACT: The authors "adapt a variant of the Comprehensive Modal Emission Model (CMEM) to show that the optimal speed and the greenest path are slope dependent (dynamic)." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

FACT: "When there are no elevation changes in a road network, the most fuel-efficient path is the shortest path with a constant (static) optimal speed." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

FACT: "[I]f the network is not flat, then the shortest path is not necessarily the greenest path, and the optimal driving speed is dynamic." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

The model transitions from static (fixed-speed, shortest-path) optimisation to dynamic (variable-speed, terrain-adapted) optimisation. The CMEM framework is the established emissions modelling standard used across the research.

**The asymptotic load finding.** FACT: The research proves "that the greenest path converges to an asymptotic greenest path as the payload approaches infinity and that this limiting path is attained for a finite load." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

*Analytical inference:* The practical implication for fleet operations is that routing optimisation for a loaded truck need not wait for full-load data — a moderate payload is sufficient for the greenest path to reach its stable, load-independent form, which simplifies real-world routing system design.

**Key terrain determinants.** FACT: "the main determinants of the CO2 emissions reduction potential are the variation of the road gradients along the shortest path as well as the relative elevation of the source from the destination." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

FACT: The MIT Sustainable Transportation page states "high-resolution elevation data reshapes the paths of diverse trucks for maximum fuel efficiency" and identifies "road gradient fluctuations and relative elevation as key factors shaping a sustainable future." *Source: MIT Sustainable Supply Chain Lab — Sustainable Transportation, MIT CTL, 2025. https://sustainable.mit.edu/sustainable-transportation.

**Traffic and downhill findings.** FACT: "[S]electing the greenest paths instead of the fastest paths can significantly reduce [CO2]." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

FACT: "while speed optimization on uphill arcs can significantly help CO2 reduction, the potential to leverage gravity for acceleration on downhill arcs is limited due to traffic congestion." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

*Analytical inference:* The downhill congestion constraint is operationally significant: regenerative braking and gravity-assisted speed gains — approaches often assumed in electric truck energy modelling — are constrained in congested urban networks, meaning the fuel/energy savings from terrain are asymmetric. Uphill speed management yields more than downhill recovery.

**Geographic scope.** FACT: The authors "use the geospatial data of 25 major cities across [6 continents]" for numerical experiments. *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

**Methodology scope and limitations.** The study is an optimisation and control paper (math.OC, with econometrics secondary classification). It demonstrates CO2 reduction potential of the routing policies relative to benchmark policies that ignore elevation. The paper benchmarks greenest-path policies against shortest-path and fastest-path policies; it does not quantify fleet-level savings in absolute tonnes for any specific operator. Specific percentage CO2 reduction figures for individual cities are not reproduced in the source blocks provided.

*Specific CO2 reduction percentage figures for individual cities not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-05.*

---

## 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**For workspaces operating road freight as a primary or secondary mode.** FACT: The research is conducted under the MIT Sustainable Supply Chain Lab, described as "a project of MIT CTL," with direct application to supply chain management. *Source: MIT Sustainable Supply Chain Lab — Sustainable Transportation, MIT CTL, 2025. https://sustainable.mit.edu/sustainable-transportation.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces where road is a primary or significant mode — handling cargo such as time-critical shipments between air gateways and client sites, or overland legs connecting ports to inland destinations — current routing systems that optimise for shortest distance or fastest time are not optimised for minimum fuel consumption when those routes cross variable terrain. The gap between current-state routing and greenest-path routing is the quantifiable emissions reduction opportunity this research maps.

*Operational implication:* The finding that the greenest path diverges from the shortest path "even for moderate payloads" means partial loads — common in high-value, low-density cargo categories such as fine art, live event equipment, or humanitarian aid — are already in the regime where terrain-aware routing changes the optimal path. The benefit is not reserved for fully loaded general-cargo trucks.

*Analytical inference:* The emphasis on high-resolution elevation data as the enabling input suggests that routing systems lacking this data layer are structurally incapable of implementing the finding, regardless of route optimisation sophistication in other dimensions. The operational gap is a data infrastructure gap as much as an algorithm gap.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces under Scope 3 emissions reporting obligations — whether voluntary under GHG Protocol, or emerging under CSRD supply chain disclosure requirements — terrain-aware fuel modelling produces more accurate upstream and downstream transport emissions estimates than distance-or-time-based proxies. The research directly supports more defensible Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transport) and Category 9 (downstream transport) calculations.

**Cargo vertical specificity.** The research applies to trucks of varying types ("diverse trucks" is the paper's own language). *Analytical inference:* The finding is directly relevant to road legs carrying any cargo vertical the workspace operates — whether specialist event freight, high-value automotive components, or humanitarian cargo moving overland. The terrain-dependence finding is vertical-agnostic; its magnitude varies by route geography, not by cargo type.

---

## 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Routing system procurement and specification.** *Analytical inference:* Workspaces evaluating or procuring transport management systems (TMS) or route optimisation tools should add high-resolution elevation data integration as an explicit evaluation criterion. A TMS that routes on time or distance without terrain input cannot implement the emissions savings this research quantifies. This is a concrete procurement specification change, not a generic "monitor" action.

**Emissions claims and green product positioning.** *Analytical inference:* Workspaces making or considering claims about "optimised" or "low-emission" road routing should examine whether their routing systems incorporate terrain-aware dynamic speed optimisation. A claim of routing efficiency that rests solely on shortest-path or fastest-path logic is not equivalent to greenest-path logic on non-flat networks — and this research provides a published, peer-reviewed basis for that distinction. Overclaiming on routing efficiency without terrain-aware methodology is a greenwashing exposure.

**Carrier and subcontractor evaluation.** *Analytical inference:* For workspaces that subcontract road legs to carriers, the question of whether those carriers employ terrain-aware routing is now a due-diligence question with published academic backing. Inserting this as a supplier evaluation criterion — alongside Euro emission standards and fleet age — is a commercially defensible position.

**Scope 3 reporting methodology.** *Analytical inference:* The CMEM variant used in this research is a recognised emission modelling framework. For workspaces moving toward activity-based (rather than spend-based) Scope 3 transport emissions calculations, the finding that elevation and gradient are "main determinants of CO2 emissions reduction potential" supports using terrain-corrected fuel consumption models rather than flat-road distance averages. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether terrain-corrected CMEM-variant modelling satisfies the ISO 14083 or GHG Protocol requirements for Scope 3 Category 4/9 emissions calculation methodology requires verification against those standards' current guidance.

**The NYC rush-hour finding and urban operations.** FACT: "selecting the greenest paths instead of the fastest paths can significantly reduce [CO2]" was tested using "speed data estimates for rush hour in New York City." *Source: arXiv:2306.01687, Moradi, Arts & Velázquez-Martínez, 30 May 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.01687.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating urban drayage or last-mile delivery in hilly cities — including but not limited to New York — the research provides direct evidence that greenest-path routing diverges from fastest-path routing even under congested conditions, and that the benefit is obtainable under real traffic conditions, not just idealised models.

---

## 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- "MIT CTL research published in 2023 and revised through May 2025 demonstrates that for trucks on non-flat road networks, the fuel-optimal route diverges from the shortest or fastest route from moderate load levels onward. We evaluate terrain-aware routing options when specifying road legs." *(Claim supported by sourced finding; appropriate for client conversations.)*
- "The research identifies road gradient variation and relative elevation as the primary determinants of how much CO2 reduction terrain-aware routing can deliver. Routes with significant elevation change offer the largest savings opportunity." *(Sourced from arXiv:2306.01687.)*
- "On downhill segments in congested urban networks, the research finds that gravity-assisted gains are limited by traffic. Speed management on uphill arcs is the more reliable lever." *(Sourced.)*

**Questions to pose to road carriers and TMS providers:**
- Does your routing optimisation incorporate high-resolution elevation data, and at what resolution?
- Is speed optimisation on individual road arcs dynamic (terrain-adjusted) or static?
- Can you produce per-shipment CO2 estimates based on terrain-corrected fuel models rather than distance averages?

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not claim specific percentage emissions reductions for specific routes without carrier-provided, route-specific data. The paper's city-level numerical results are not reproduced in the available source blocks, and the paper itself reports research benchmarks under modelled conditions, not fleet operational outcomes.
- Do not represent the arXiv preprint as a published journal article in marketing materials. The arXiv record carries a Related DOI pointing to a Springer OR journal record, but the journal publication status was not confirmable from the source blocks provided. *Specific publication date and journal citation status not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-05.*
- Do not conflate terrain-aware routing with zero-emission trucking. The research addresses internal combustion truck fuel efficiency; its direct application to BEV trucks involves additional energy recovery and powertrain considerations not addressed in the available source blocks.

---

## 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**Electric vehicle and alternative powertrain applicability.** The CMEM framework is calibrated for conventional internal combustion trucks. *Analytical inference:* Application to BEV trucks requires extension of the model to account for regenerative braking energy recovery, battery state-of-charge dynamics, and charging infrastructure constraints — none of which are addressed in the available source blocks. The downhill congestion finding (limited gravity-assisted gains in urban networks) is directionally relevant to BEV energy recovery, but the model does not quantify BEV-specific outcomes.

**Fleet-level deployment and operational validation.** The research is a mathematical optimisation study using geospatial simulation data. It does not report outcomes from fleet operational trials. *Analytical inference:* Translation of the academic finding into verified fleet-level CO2 reduction figures requires real-world implementation studies, which are not reported in the available source blocks.

**Quantified CO2 reduction figures for specific corridors.** The paper reports numerical benchmarks for 25 cities but the specific figures are not in the source blocks available to this brief. Workspaces seeking to use the findings for specific route-level claims will need to access the full paper.

**Scope 3 regulatory standard compatibility.** Whether CMEM-variant terrain-corrected modelling satisfies ISO 14083 or GHG Protocol Scope 3 Category 4/9 methodology requirements is not addressed in the source material. *Legal Confirmation Required:* compatibility of terrain-aware CMEM-based fuel estimates with current Scope 3 accounting standards requires specialist review.

**The Sustainable Transportation Consortium's ongoing work.** The MIT CTL maintains an active Sustainable Transportation Consortium (https://sustainable.mit.edu/transportation-consort/) described as covering route optimisation, Scope 3 standards, and empty-miles reduction. The consortium's current membership, active projects, and phase status were not available in the source blocks provided. *Specific [consortium membership, active project status, current phase] not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-05.* This is a forward-intelligence gap: if the consortium is testing terrain-aware routing with industry partners, that is material intelligence. Retrieval recommended; see New Sources Identified.

**Journal publication status.** The arXiv record references a Related DOI at doi.org/10.1007/s00291-024-00793-9 (Springer OR journal), but the publication status of the final peer-reviewed version was not confirmable from the source blocks. *Analytical inference:* If the journal version is now published, it supersedes the preprint for citation purposes and may contain revised numerical results from v2. Monitor for journal version confirmation.

*Re-check window: Q4 2025 — confirm journal publication via Springer OR DOI; check MIT CTL Sustainable Transportation Consortium for new industry partner results or phase updates; check for regulatory citation of CMEM-variant methodology in ISO 14083 or GHG Protocol guidance revisions.*

---
